hook: You already know how to estimate parameters using data alone — but what if you had useful prior knowledge before collecting a single observation? Classical statistics leaves that prior knowledge on the table, and Bayesian inference is the framework that puts it to work. How should rational beliefs update in the face of new evidence, and how do we quantify uncertainty once they do?
concept: The unifying idea is that probability describes degrees of belief, not just long-run frequencies — so parameters themselves have distributions that we update as data arrives, captured formally by Bayes' theorem: $p(\theta \mid x) \propto p(x \mid \theta)\, p(\theta)$.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn how to encode prior beliefs about a parameter as a distribution and update it into a posterior distribution using observed data and Bayes' theorem.
Master how to choose a conjugate prior so that the posterior belongs to the same distributional family, making Bayesian updating analytically tractable for common likelihood models.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Bayesian Point Estimation and Credible Intervals
topic_roadmap.description: Apply the posterior distribution to produce point estimates (posterior mean, median, or MAP) and credible intervals that carry a direct probability interpretation about where the parameter lies.
You will learn how to use resampling to construct confidence intervals when closed-form expressions are unavailable, bridging Bayesian intuition with a flexible, data-driven approach.
